text: Hello dear viewer, my name is Dr. Vishal Soni and today we would be watching a case of sleeve gastrectomy done in a case of morbid obesity. I would skip through the clinical details because this is more of a technical video. If you are a surgeon, you would find all the steps that are needed to perform the surgery and if you are a patient, you would see what exactly happens during your surgery when I operate you using the surgical robot.

The surgical setup includes a cadier forceps in the surgeon's right hand for retraction, a vessel sealer as the active instrument on the right side, and a fenestrated bipolar in the left hand. A Nathanson retractor is positioned to elevate the left lobe of the liver.

The surgeon separates the greater omentum from the greater curvature of the stomach, carefully preserving the gastroepiploic vessels while ensuring precise hemostasis. The vessel sealer and fenestrated bipolar are used to manage potential bleeding from vessels encountered during the dissection.

The surgeon carefully separates the greater omentum from the greater curvature of the stomach, progressing towards the gastroesophageal junction. The short gastric vessels are encountered and efficiently taken down using a vessel sealer, ensuring precise hemostasis to prevent potential bleeding.

In last couple of minutes, you must have seen the way in which the instruments, especially the vessel sealer, it is moving just the way my hand would move in someone's abdomen. And this is what robotic surgery is all about. As opposed to the stress of using a straight stick instrument in laparoscopic surgery, where we try and achieve the same steps, The concepts of the surgery essentially remain the same. But the stress of achieving the same, I believe, is more than ten times. But here, because my instrument can bend properly, it can go into any corner of what I'm seeing. This gives me ability to achieve the most precise step that I have thought of for my patient. And in the most trickiest of the steps, I know that I'll be able to achieve it. And that is the beauty of robotic surgery.

The surgeon uses superior instruments to pull down the last portion of the greater omentum from the greater curvature of the stomach, ensuring complete detachment of the greater curvature and fundus. The procedure is performed in a confined space below the enlarged left lobe of the liver near the gastroesophageal junction.

The surgeon focuses on the retrogastric tissues, which appear less thick than expected. Using a vessel sealer, the surgeon precisely dissects flimsy adhesions. The fundus and gastroesophageal junction are freed from attachments at the splenic pole in preparation for stapling and removing the redundant portion of the stomach.

The surgeon adjusts the position of the instrument to ensure precise firing at the gastroesophageal junction. Once satisfied, the focus shifts to the lower part of the stomach, moving towards the pylorus. The separation is carried out up to five centimeters away from the pylorus in preparation for stapling.

The surgeon flexes the vessel sealer with precision, maneuvering it to perform hemostatic actions that remove the omentum from the surgical field and away from the greater curvature of the stomach. The remaining section is approximately five centimeters from the pyloric region.

The surgeon introduces a SureForm robotic stapler, which articulates to facilitate precise stapling of the stomach. A bougie is in place to prevent excessive narrowing, guiding the creation of a gastric sleeve. Indicators on the screen show the stapling process is in progress, with the first and second fires executed to ensure precise apposition of the anterior and posterior stomach walls.

The surgeon flexes the robotic staplers towards the left side of the screen, following the natural curvature of the stomach to create a sleeve. The staple line is progressively adjusted to ensure precision, aiming to reach the gastroesophageal junction and properly divide the fundus. The belt of fat at the gastroesophageal junction serves as a visual marker for staple line placement.

The surgeon maneuvers the robotic camera to ensure precise stapling of the stomach while a bougie is in place to prevent narrowing. The robotic stapler is tilted leftward to align with the natural curvature of the stomach, and the bell's east part of fat is identified as a marker near the gastroesophageal junction. The final stapling is completed, marking the end of the sleeve gastrectomy. The next step involves the installation of diluted methylene blue to check for leaks.

The surgeon performs a leak test by instilling 100 to 120 mL of diluted methylene blue into the newly created sleeve gastrectomy. The test confirms the anastomosis is waterproof and leak-proof, while also providing a visual indication of the sleeve size.
